title_en: Chapter on Whoever Fulfills Tawhid Shall Enter Paradise Without Account
title_ar: باب من حقّق التوحيد دخل الجنة بغير حساب
content_en:
This chapter presents one of the most inspiring passages in all of Islamic literature — the famous hadith of the seventy thousand who shall enter Paradise without reckoning. Ibn Abd al-Wahhab uses this narration to define with precision what it truly means to 'fulfill' or 'perfect' Tawhid, a standard that goes far beyond mere verbal declaration and touches the innermost convictions and outward behaviors of the believer.

The hadith in question is reported by Ibn Abbas and recorded in the collections of both al-Bukhari and Muslim. In it, the Prophet ﷺ describes his vision of the nations of past prophets and then his own community on the Day of Judgment. He says: 'I was shown the nations, and I saw a prophet with a small group, a prophet with one or two men, and a prophet with no one. Then I was shown a great multitude and I thought it was my nation, but I was told: this is Musa and his people. Then look over there — and I saw a great multitude covering the horizon. I was told: this is your nation, and among them are seventy thousand who shall enter Paradise without reckoning and without punishment.' The Companions were filled with curiosity about who these seventy thousand were, and the Prophet ﷺ described them as those who 'do not use ruqyah (impermissible incantations), do not see evil omens in things, do not use branding, and who place their trust in their Lord.'

The scholar Ukkasheh ibn Mihsan then stood and asked to be among them, and the Prophet ﷺ confirmed that he would be. When another man made the same request, the Prophet ﷺ informed him gently that Ukkasheh had preceded him.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's analysis of this hadith focuses on the four characteristics mentioned. The avoidance of impermissible incantations (not the Quran and prophetic supplications, which are permissible), the rejection of superstitious omens, the abandonment of cauterization as a first resort, and — most crucially — complete reliance on Allah (tawakkul) together constitute the highest practical expression of Tawhid. Each of these traits is, at its root, an expression of the heart's singular dependence on Allah. The one who sees bad omens in a black cat, or who cannot function without consulting a fortune-teller, has allowed something other than Allah to govern their trust and hope.

This chapter also contains an important scholarly discussion on the permissibility of various forms of ruqyah. The hadith does not prohibit all incantations — only those that involve calling upon other than Allah, using unknown or polytheistic formulas, or that are accompanied by the belief that the words themselves have inherent power independent of Allah's permission. The Prophet ﷺ himself performed and permitted ruqyah using Quranic verses and authentic supplications. What is condemned here is the type that entails reliance upon other than Allah or superstitious beliefs incompatible with sound Tawhid.

The aspiration of this chapter is to motivate the believer toward the highest level of Tawhid — not the minimum sufficient for salvation, but the perfection of reliance, trust, and dependence on Allah that characterized the best of the Prophet's ﷺ Companions. This is Tawhid not merely as an intellectual affirmation but as a living reality that restructures the heart's orientation toward the world.
